inputs.story: Help the Secretary of State sort out which of her diplomats she needs to send out next. Determine the month and destination of each diplomat, and the duration of their visit. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. Vasquez will leave sometime after Macdonald.
2. Vasquez will leave 1 month before the ambassador with the 5 day visit.
3. Gilbert is either the person leaving in January or the ambassador with the 4 day visit.
4. Macdonald will leave 1 month before the ambassador with the 4 day visit.
5. The ambassador with the 4 day visit will leave sometime before the ambassador with the 9 day visit.
inputs.constants:
diplomats: Gilbert; Macdonald; Pickett; Vasquez.
months: January; February; March; April.
durations: 4 day; 5 day; 9 day; 10 day.
inputs.solution:
Months	Diplomats	Durations
January	Macdonald	10 day
February	Gilbert	4 day
March	Vasquez	9 day
April	Pickett	5 day

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
Help the Secretary of State sort out which of her diplomats she needs to send out next. Determine the month and destination of each diplomat, and the duration of their visit. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Vasquez will leave sometime after Macdonald.
2. Vasquez will leave 1 month before the ambassador with the 5 day visit.
3. Gilbert is either the person leaving in January or the ambassador with the 4 day visit.
4. Macdonald will leave 1 month before the ambassador with the 4 day visit.
5. The ambassador with the 4 day visit will leave sometime before the ambassador with the 9 day visit.

Constants:
diplomats: Gilbert; Macdonald; Pickett; Vasquez.
months: January; February; March; April.
durations: 4 day; 5 day; 9 day; 10 day.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

ASP Program:
asp_generation.thinking:
Here's a thinking process:

1.  **Understand the Goal**: The user wants an ASP (Answer Set Programming) program to solve a logic grid puzzle. The program should use Clingo to find a unique solution based on the given story, clues, and constants.

2.  **Analyze the Problem**:
   - **Categories**:
     - Diplomats: Gilbert, Macdonald, Pickett, Vasquez
     - Months: January, February, March, April
     - Durations: 4 day, 5 day, 9 day, 10 day
   - **Clues**:
     1. Vasquez will leave sometime after Macdonald. (Month(Vasquez) > Month(Macdonald))
     2. Vasquez will leave 1 month before the ambassador with the 5 day visit. (Month(Vasquez) + 1 = Month(5 day))
     3. Gilbert is either the person leaving in January or the ambassador with the 4 day visit. (Month(Gilbert)=January OR Duration(Gilbert)=4 day)
     4. Macdonald will leave 1 month before the ambassador with the 4 day visit. (Month(Macdonald) + 1 = Month(4 day))
     5. The ambassador with the 4 day visit will leave sometime before the ambassador with the 9 day visit. (Month(4 day) < Month(9 day))
   - **Constraints**: Each diplomat gets exactly one month and one duration. No duplicates in months or durations across diplomats.

3.  **Map Constants to ASP Format**:
   - `diplomat("Gilbert"; "Macdonald"; "Pickett"; "Vasquez").`
   - `month("January"; "February"; "March"; "April").` (Keep as strings, need to handle ordering later)
   - `duration(4; 5; 9; 10).` (Strip " day", keep integers)
   - Wait, rule 1 says: "Prefer integers: strip special characters... convert verbal or ordinal numerals... Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible". Months are typically strings, but I could map them to integers 1,2,3,4 for easier comparison. However, the examples keep strings for categories like wood_type and country. I'll stick to strings for months, but I need to handle ordering. Clingo doesn't have built-in string ordering for months. I should map months to integers for comparison, or define an ordering explicitly. Actually, I can just use integers for months: `month(1; 2; 3; 4).` and then map them to names if needed, or just keep strings and use explicit ordering rules. But Clingo's `>` works on numbers. It's easier to use integers for months: 1=January, 2=February, 3=March, 4=April. I'll define `month_num(1; 2; 3; 4).` and keep `month_name("January"; "February"; "March"; "April").` but the puzzle just asks to determine month and duration. I'll stick to strings for months in the predicate, but use a helper mapping for comparison, or just use integers directly and label them. Actually, the examples use strings for categories that don't need arithmetic. But clues 1, 2, 4, 5 involve ordering/time differences. I'll convert months to integers for simplicity: `month(1; 2; 3; 4).` and maybe add a comment or just use the integers. Wait, the prompt says "Prefer integers: strip special characters...". I'll convert months to integers 1..4. But to be safe and match the prompt's style, I'll keep them as strings but define an ordering relation or just use integers. Let's stick to integers for months: `month(1; 2; 3; 4).` with a comment mapping them, or just use strings and encode ordering explicitly: `less_than(Jan, Feb)`, etc. Actually, it's simpler to just use integers 1-4 for months and note the mapping, or keep strings and use a comparison predicate. I'll use integers for months to leverage Clingo's arithmetic.
   Wait, the prompt says: "Convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9)." So January=1, February=2, March=3, April=4 is perfectly aligned with the rule! I'll use `month(1; 2; 3; 4).`
   Durations: `duration(4; 5; 9; 10).`
   Diplomats: `diplomat("Gilbert"; "Macdonald"; "Pickett"; "Vasquez").`

   Let's define the main predicate: `assign(D, M, Dura).` where D is diplomat, M is month (1-4), Dura is duration (4,5,9,10).

4.  **Formulate ASP Rules**:
   - **Constants**:
     ```asp
     diplomat("Gilbert"; "Macdonald"; "Pickett"; "Vasquez").
     month(1; 2; 3; 4).
     duration(4; 5; 9; 10).
     ```
   - **Search Space**:
     ```asp
     {assign(D, M, Dura): month(M), duration(Dura)} = 1 :- diplomat(D).
     ```
   - **Uniqueness**:
     ```asp
     {D1=D2; M1=M2; Dura1=Dura2}=0 :- assign(D1,M1,Dura1), assign(D2,M2,Dura2), (D1,M1,Dura1)!=(D2,M2,Dura2).
     ```
   - **Clue 1**: Vasquez will leave sometime after Macdonald.
     `M_vas > M_mac :- assign("Vasquez", M_vas, _), assign("Macdonald", M_mac, _).`
   - **Clue 2**: Vasquez will leave 1 month before the ambassador with the 5 day visit.
     `M_vas + 1 = M_5 :- assign("Vasquez", M_vas, _), assign(_, M_5, 5).`
   - **Clue 3**: Gilbert is either the person leaving in January or the ambassador with the 4 day visit.
     `{M=1; Dura=4}=1 :- assign("Gilbert", M, Dura).`
   - **Clue 4**: Macdonald will leave 1 month before the ambassador with the 4 day visit.
     `M_mac + 1 = M_4 :- assign("Macdonald", M_mac, _), assign(_, M_4, 4).`
   - **Clue 5**: The ambassador with the 4 day visit will leave sometime before the ambassador with the 9 day visit.
     `M_4 < M_9 :- assign(_, M_4, 4), assign(_, M_9, 9).`

   Wait, Clue 2 says "1 month before". If months are 1,2,3,4, then `M_vas + 1 = M_5` works.
   Let's check consistency:
   From 4: Macdonald is 1 month before 4-day visit. So M_mac + 1 = M_4.
   From 2: Vasquez is 1 month before 5-day visit. So M_vas + 1 = M_5.
   From 1: M_vas > M_mac.
   From 5: M_4 < M_9.
   Let's solve manually to verify:
   Months: 1, 2, 3, 4.
   M_mac + 1 = M_4 => M_mac can be 1, 2, 3. M_4 can be 2, 3, 4.
   M_vas + 1 = M_5 => M_vas can be 1, 2, 3. M_5 can be 2, 3, 4.
   M_vas > M_mac.
   If M_mac = 1, M_4 = 2.
   If M_mac = 2, M_4 = 3.
   If M_mac = 3, M_4 = 4.
   M_vas > M_mac.
   Suppose M_mac = 1. Then M_vas can be 2 or 3.
   If M_vas = 2, M_5 = 3.
   If M_vas = 3, M_5 = 4.
   Check Clue 5: M_4 < M_9.
   Case A: M_mac=1, M_4=2. M_vas=2 -> conflict (Vasquez and Macdonald can't have same month). So M_vas must be > M_mac.
   If M_mac=1, M_4=2. M_vas can be 3 (since M_vas > 1). Then M_5 = 4.

   Months used: 1 (Mac), 3 (Vas). Remaining: 2, 4.
   M_4 = 2. So whoever has duration 4 is in month 2.
   M_5 = 4. So whoever has duration 5 is in month 4.
   Clue 5: M_4 (2) < M_9. So M_9 can be 3 or 4. But month 4 is taken by duration 5. So M_9 must be 3.
   So duration 9 is in month 3. But month 3 is Vasquez. So Vasquez has duration 9.
   Let's check durations: 4-day is month 2. 5-day is month 4. 9-day is month 3. Remaining duration is 10-day, must be month 1 (Macdonald).
   So: Macdonald (1, 10-day), Gilbert/Pickett (2, 4-day), Vasquez (3, 9-day), Pickett/Gilbert (4, 5-day).
   Check Clue 3: Gilbert is either month 1 or duration 4.
   Month 1 is Macdonald. So Gilbert must be duration 4.
   So Gilbert is month 2, duration 4.
   Then Pickett is month 4, duration 5.
   Let's verify all clues:
   1. Vasquez (3) after Macdonald (1)? Yes.
   2. Vasquez (3) 1 month before 5-day (4)? Yes.
   3. Gilbert is month 1 or 4-day? Gilbert is 4-day. Yes.
   4. Macdonald (1) 1 month before 4-day (2)? Yes.
   5. 4-day (2) before 9-day (3)? Yes.
   All consistent. Unique solution.

   The ASP encoding looks solid.

5.  **Refine ASP Code**:
   Let's structure it carefully according to the rules.
   - Constants: `diplomat/1`, `month/1`, `duration/1`.
   - Predicate: `assign(D, M, D)`. Wait, `D` is used for diplomat and duration. I'll use `dur` for duration to avoid confusion. `assign(Diplomat, Month, Duration)`.
   - Search space: `{assign(D, M, Dur): month(M), duration(Dur)} = 1 :- diplomat(D).`
   - Uniqueness: `{D1=D2; M1=M2; Dur1=Dur2}=0 :- assign(D1,M1,Dur1), assign(D2,M2,Dur2), (D1,M1,Dur1)!=(D2,M2,Dur2).`
   - Clue 1: `M_v > M_m :- assign("Vasquez", M_v, _), assign("Macdonald", M_m, _).`
   - Clue 2: `M_v + 1 = M_5 :- assign("Vasquez", M_v, _), assign(_, M_5, 5).`
   - Clue 3: `{M=1; Dur=4}=1 :- assign("Gilbert", M, Dur).`
   - Clue 4: `M_m + 1 = M_4 :- assign("Macdonald", M_m, _), assign(_, M_4, 4).`
   - Clue 5: `M_4 < M_9 :- assign(_, M_4, 4), assign(_, M_9, 9).`
   - Show directive: `#show assign/3.`

   The prompt says:
   `<C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`
   `{<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`
   `:- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`

   For Clue 1 & 4 & 5, I used `M_v > M_m :- ...`. This is valid ASP (head is an atom or constraint, but here it's a constraint `:- M_v <= M_m.` or I can use `:- not (M_v > M_m).` Actually, Clingo allows arithmetic in head: `M_v > M_m :- ...` is valid. Or I can write it as a constraint: `:- assign("Vasquez", M_v, _), assign("Macdonald", M_m, _), not (M_v > M_m).` But Clingo supports `M_v > M_m :- ...` directly. I'll stick to `M_v > M_m :- ...` as it's standard in examples (e.g., `P1>P2 :- ...`).

diplomat("Gilbert"; "Macdonald"; "Pickett"; "Vasquez").
month(1; 2; 3; 4).
duration(4; 5; 9; 10).

% Search space: for each diplomat D, assign exactly one month M and duration Dur.
{assign(D, M, Dur): month(M), duration(Dur)} = 1 :- diplomat(D).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.

% 1. Vasquez will leave sometime after Macdonald.
M_v > M_m :- assign("Vasquez", M_v, _), assign("Macdonald", M_m, _).

% 2. Vasquez will leave 1 month before the ambassador with the 5 day visit.
M_v + 1 = M_5 :- assign("Vasquez", M_v, _), assign(_, M_5, 5).

% 3. Gilbert is either the person leaving in January or the ambassador with the 4 day visit.
{M=1; Dur=4}=1 :- assign("Gilbert", M, Dur).

% 4. Macdonald will leave 1 month before the ambassador with the 4 day visit.
M_m + 1 = M_4 :- assign("Macdonald", M_m, _), assign(_, M_4, 4).

% 5. The ambassador with the 4 day visit will leave sometime before the ambassador with the 9 day visit.
M_4 < M_9 :- assign(_, M_4, 4), assign(_, M_9, 9).
